title: A Two-Model Approach for Humour Style Recognition
research_questions: How can a text dataset be created for recognising self-enhancing, self-deprecating, affiliative, aggressive, and neutral text?
How well do classic machine learning classifiers, sentence-embedding models, and DistilBERT perform on humour style recognition?
Does a two-model approach that first combines affiliative/aggressive humour and then separates them improve humour style recognition, particularly affiliative humour?

datasets_used.name: Humour Styles Dataset
datasets_used.size: 1,463 instances: 298 self-enhancing, 265 self-deprecating, 250 affiliative, 318 aggressive, and 332 neutral; text lengths ranging from 4 to 229 words

datasets_used.name: Short Text Corpus
datasets_used.size: 200 instances consisting of 150 jokes and 50 non-jokes

methods_proposed.name: Two-model approach for humour style recognition
methods_proposed.summary: The method first trains a four-class classifier for self-enhancing, self-deprecating, neutral, and a combined affiliative/aggressive class. A second binary classifier is then applied to the combined class to distinguish affiliative from aggressive humour.

humor_theories.detail: The classification labels are based on Martin et al. (2003): self-enhancing, self-deprecating, affiliative, and aggressive humour, with neutral added for non-humorous text.
The introduction describes relief theory as highlighting humour's role in relaxation.
The paper describes incongruity theory as finding something funny when expectations mismatch what actually happens.
Aggressive humour is described as rooted in superiority theory and as belittling or mocking others.
Play theory is mentioned as an evolutionary-psychology account of laughter as a non-aggressive play signal.

evaluation_methods.detail: Six Ph.D. candidates from Africa, Asia, and Europe annotated two sets of 100 Short Text Corpus samples; Fleiss’ Kappa was 0.2651 for the first set and 0.2841 for the second set.
Chat-GPT, Claude, Microsoft Copilot, and HuggingChat were used to classify the 14 instances where all three human annotators disagreed, after which a majority label was assigned across seven labels.
Models were evaluated with accuracy, precision, recall, and f1-score using an 80/20 train/test split with fixed seed 100 and 5-fold cross-validation.
A Wilcoxon signed-rank test compared the single-model and two-model approaches across metrics and humour styles.

headline_results.finding: The best single-model five-class result in Table 4 achieved 77.8% accuracy and 77.3% f1-score.
The best individual four-class model achieved 85.3% accuracy and 85.1% macro-mean f1-score.
The best individual binary-class model achieved 80.0% accuracy and 80.0% f1-score.
The best two-model combination achieved 78.0% f1-score and 77.8% accuracy.
headline_results.best_system: MUL + XGBoost four-class model + ALI + XGBoost binary-class model

headline_results.finding: Across the 14 models, the two-model approach increased average f1-score from 70.52 to 75.43 and average accuracy from 71.83 to 75.25.
Affiliative humour classification improved by 11.61% in f1-score, with 14 of 14 models improving and p-value 0.0001.

key_findings: The paper introduces a 1,463-instance English text dataset for four humour styles plus neutral text, with class counts ranging from 250 affiliative to 332 neutral instances.
Single-model five-class classifiers performed reasonably overall but consistently struggled with affiliative humour, whose f1-scores ranged from 39.2% to 64.9%.
The proposed two-model approach improves overall average performance across models, raising average f1-score from 70.52 to 75.43 and average accuracy from 71.83 to 75.25.
The largest gain is for affiliative humour: average f1-score increased by 11.61%, and all 14 tested models improved on that class.
Aggressive humour did not show a statistically significant improvement under the two-model approach; its average difference was -1.65 with p-value 0.1189.
limitations: The dataset has only 1,463 instances, which the paper says may limit model generalisation.
Humour annotation is subjective; Fleiss’ Kappa was 0.2651 and 0.2841 for the two annotated sets, and some instances required LLM-assisted tie-breaking.
The focus on English-centric jokes may introduce language and cultural biases.
Most websites except Reader’s Digest and Laugh Factory featured jokes from only one humour type, potentially leading classifiers to learn spurious source-specific correlations.
The paper is text-only; future work is suggested for multimodal cues, personalised models, larger multilingual datasets, and generative models.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper frames humour style recognition as useful for mental health research and content moderation, including identifying potentially harmful aggressive or self-deprecating humour, while noting cultural and language biases in the English-centric dataset.

human_samples.recruitment: Recruited to serve as annotators; no further recruitment details reported.
human_samples.observations: 200 Short Text Corpus instances divided into two sets of 100; each set independently annotated by 3 annotators. Fleiss’ Kappa: 0.2651 for first 100 samples and 0.2841 for second 100 samples. First set: 91 samples had at least two annotators agreeing and 9 had all three disagreeing; second set: 95 had at least two agreeing and 5 had all three disagreeing.
human_samples.detail: Annotators were provided humour-style definitions and asked to classify each instance as self-enhancing, self-deprecating, aggressive, affiliative, or neutral. Majority vote determined the final label where possible.

human_samples.population: Website users or editors who supplied original labels, definitions, or tags on online joke websites
human_samples.observations: 983 jokes extracted from websites including Reader’s Digest, Parade, Bored Panda, Laugh Factory, Pun Me, Independent, Cracked, Reddit, Tastefully Offensive, and BuzzFeed; the paper says jokes were labelled by users or editors and then mapped to the study’s humour-style categories.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report the number, identity, demographics, or procedures of the website users/editors. Authors mapped website keywords/tags such as dark/inappropriate jokes, insult, friendship, family jokes, self-deprecating, self-love, and self-care to the five study labels.

judges.name: Six Ph.D. candidate annotators
judges.detail: Three human annotators labeled each of two 100-item Short Text Corpus subsets; majority vote assigned final labels except for 14 all-disagreement cases.

judges.name: Chat-GPT
judges.detail: Used to classify the 14 Short Text Corpus instances where all three human annotators disagreed; its label was combined with the three human labels and labels from three other LLM chatbots for a seven-label majority decision.

judges.name: Claude
judges.detail: Used to classify the 14 all-human-disagreement instances; exact version/snapshot not reported.

judges.name: Microsoft Copilot
judges.detail: Used to classify the 14 all-human-disagreement instances; exact version/snapshot not reported.

judges.name: HuggingChat
judges.detail: Used to classify the 14 all-human-disagreement instances; exact model/version not reported.

judges.name: Website users or editors
judges.detail: Original website labels, definitions, or tags determined labels for the 983 website jokes before authors mapped them to humour-style categories; the paper does not identify whether specific labels were user- or editor-generated.

judges.name: Paper authors
judges.detail: Mapped website tags/keywords and source categories to the study’s labels, including mapping dark/inappropriate jokes and insults to aggressive, friendship/family/classroom/icebreaker/happiness jokes to affiliative, self-deprecating tags to self-deprecating, self-love/self-care to self-enhancing, and ColBERT non-humorous instances to neutral.

judges.name: Accuracy, precision, recall, f1-score
judges.detail: Used to evaluate classifier performance on the Humour Styles Dataset for the single-model, four-class, binary-class, and two-model approaches.

judges.name: Wilcoxon signed-rank test
judges.detail: Used to compare single-model and two-model approaches across metrics and humour styles.

generation_selection.detail: The study performs dataset construction, annotation, and text classification; it does not generate humour or select among generated outputs.

model_provenance.name: Naive Bayes
model_provenance.detail: Run as one of 14 evaluated classifiers; smoothing parameter reported as 1, but software/package/version not reported.

model_provenance.name: Random Forest
model_provenance.detail: Run with six sentence-embedding models; default hyperparameters used, but implementation/library version not reported.

model_provenance.name: XGBoost
model_provenance.detail: Run with six sentence-embedding models; default hyperparameters used, but implementation/library version not reported.

model_provenance.name: DistilBERT
model_provenance.detail: Fine-tuned for the classification tasks; the exact checkpoint/model ID is not reported. The paper mentions the Hugging Face Transformers library but not its version.

model_provenance.name: General Text Embeddings (GTE)
model_provenance.detail: One of six sentence embedding model families combined with RF and XGBoost; exact checkpoint not reported.

model_provenance.name: GTE Upgraded (ALI)
model_provenance.detail: One of six sentence embedding model families combined with RF and XGBoost; exact checkpoint not reported.

model_provenance.name: BAAI General Embedding (BGE)
model_provenance.detail: One of six sentence embedding model families combined with RF and XGBoost; exact checkpoint not reported.

model_provenance.name: Matryoshka Representation Learning and Binary Quantization (MRL)
model_provenance.detail: One of six sentence embedding model families combined with RF and XGBoost; exact checkpoint not reported.

model_provenance.name: Universal AnglE Embedding (UAE)
model_provenance.detail: One of six sentence embedding model families combined with RF and XGBoost; exact checkpoint not reported.

model_provenance.name: Multilingual E5 Text Embeddings (MUL)
model_provenance.detail: One of six sentence embedding model families combined with RF and XGBoost; exact checkpoint not reported.

model_provenance.name: Chat-GPT
model_provenance.detail: Used as an LLM chatbot for annotation tie-breaking on 14 disagreement instances; exact model/version not reported.

model_provenance.name: Claude
model_provenance.detail: Used as an LLM chatbot for annotation tie-breaking on 14 disagreement instances; exact model/version not reported.

model_provenance.name: Microsoft Copilot
model_provenance.detail: Used as an LLM chatbot for annotation tie-breaking on 14 disagreement instances; exact model/version not reported.

model_provenance.name: HuggingChat
model_provenance.detail: Used as an LLM chatbot for annotation tie-breaking on 14 disagreement instances; exact underlying model/version not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Four LLM chatbot classifications per unresolved item are reported for 14 items; the paper does not state whether there were repeated prompts or retries.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: One label from each of 4 LLMs plus 3 human annotator labels, yielding 7 labels per unresolved item for majority assignment.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper states that the LLMs were prompted to categorise each joke instance as self-enhancing, self-deprecating, aggressive, affiliative, or neutral. Full prompts, decoding settings, context length, and cost are not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: Single-model, four-class, binary-class, and two-model classifier experiments
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Dataset split 80/20 for training/testing, randomised with fixed seed 100; 5-fold cross-validation used for all experiments. Naive Bayes smoothing parameter = 1. Random Forest and XGBoost used default hyperparameters.
inference_budgets.detail: Fourteen evaluated models: NB, RF plus six embeddings, XGBoost plus six embeddings, and DistilBERT. The paper reports metrics but not hardware, run counts beyond 5-fold CV, or library versions.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Fine-tuned for 5 epochs with weight decay 0.01, warmup steps 500, training batch size 8, and the default learning-rate scheduler from the Hugging Face Transformers library.
inference_budgets.detail: Exact DistilBERT checkpoint, learning rate, tokenizer settings, maximum sequence length, hardware, and training time are not reported.

human_baselines.description: Human annotators were used to label 200 dataset instances from the Short Text Corpus, but the paper does not evaluate human classification performance as a baseline on the same train/test or cross-validation tasks as the models.
human_baselines.detail: No materially matched human-vs-model baseline is reported.

mitigations: The paper introduces a new 1,463-instance Humour Styles Dataset rather than evaluating on a previously released humour-style benchmark.
Supervised evaluation uses an 80/20 train/test split with fixed seed 100 and 5-fold cross-validation.
contamination_risk.basis: The full labelled benchmark is introduced in this paper, so it was not a public benchmark before the evaluated classifiers. However, many evaluated representations are pretrained language/embedding models, and the dataset is assembled from public web jokes plus existing public corpora (ColBERT and Short Text Corpus). The paper gives no collection dates, duplicate checks, or decontamination tests against pretraining data. Thus supervised train/test leakage is partly controlled, but pretraining/source-text contamination remains plausible and unmeasured.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Labels for 14 Short Text Corpus instances where all three human annotators disagreed, and any downstream classifier training/evaluation results that include those instances.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Each affected item retained the three original human annotator labels; four LLM labels were added, and the final label was assigned by majority over seven labels. No independent post hoc human validation is reported.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: LLM judging does not directly score model outputs or determine the headline comparison between single-model and two-model classifiers, but it contributes labels to a small subset of the dataset used for model training/evaluation.

reporting_gaps: Exact versions, snapshots, underlying models, and inference dates are not reported for Chat-GPT, Claude, Microsoft Copilot, or HuggingChat.
Exact checkpoint/model IDs and versions are not reported for DistilBERT or the six embedding models: GTE, ALI, BGE, MRL, UAE, and MUL.
Software/library versions are not reported for Naive Bayes, Random Forest, XGBoost, Hugging Face Transformers, or other tooling.
The LLM prompt is described only generally; full prompt text, system messages, conversation context, decoding/sampling parameters, token limits, retries, and cost are not reported.

Annotator recruitment details, compensation, training beyond receiving humour-style definitions, language proficiency, demographic details beyond broad regions, and blind/independent annotation conditions are not fully reported.

The number and characteristics of website users/editors who supplied original labels/tags are not reported, and the paper does not specify which website labels were user-generated versus editor-generated.
No dates are reported for web data collection, LLM annotation, model training, or inference.
No decontamination, duplicate-overlap, or memorisation analysis is reported for public web/source-corpus items relative to pretrained models.
Hardware, compute budget, training time, and monetary cost are not reported.
Feature extraction details for Naive Bayes and exact preprocessing/tokenization settings are not fully specified.
The paper reports an 80/20 split and 5-fold cross-validation but does not provide the train/test class distributions or all procedural details needed to reproduce every reported table exactly.
